metadata.note: Candidate 21. Avoided crossing scoped to coupled states; exact crossings remain possible when coupling vanishes or symmetry forbids mixing. No sum-over-states or matrix diagonalization derivation.

source_of_truth: beat_sheet.json — edit the sheet, not this file

beats.prompt: Start with two uncoupled states. As an external parameter changes, one energy rises and the other falls. With no communication between them, the lines cross cle

beats.prompt: Now add a perturbation that connects the states. Away from the meeting point, the effect is small because the energy separation is large.
beats.label: Now add a perturbation that connects the states. Away from the meeting point, the effect is small be

beats.prompt: Near the meeting point, the separation shrinks. Mixing becomes strong, and neither state can remain the original pure state.

beats.prompt: The closer the bare levels come, the more strongly the coupled energies split.
beats.label: The closer the bare levels come, the more strongly the coupled energies split.

beats.prompt: The lower eigenvalue bends downward while the upper one bends upward. At closest approach, the gap is twice the magnitude of the coupling g.

beats.prompt: This is an avoided crossing: the energy curves do not touch, even though the uncoupled reference lines would have crossed at the center.
beats.label: This is an avoided crossing: the energy curves do not touch, even though the uncoupled reference lin

beats.prompt: Perturbation theory gives the same intuition: energy shifts contain a gap in the denominator, so nearby coupled levels influence one another much more strongly

beats.prompt: Important exception: levels may cross exactly when their coupling is zero—for example, when symmetry places them in sectors that cannot mix.
beats.label: Important exception: levels may cross exactly when their coupling is zero—for example, when symmetry

beats.prompt: So the rule is conditional, not mystical: coupled levels repel; uncoupled levels may cross. The size of the avoided gap directly measures the coupling strength.
